_meta.authoringNotes: Pathology, inheritance, and classification per Savige J et al. Pediatr Nephrol 2019 (PMID 30298311) — Genetics of Alport Syndrome International Workshop consensus — and Kashtan CE. Cold Spring Harb Perspect Med 2024. Genotype-phenotype correlation per Jais JP et al. J Am Soc Nephrol 2000 (PMID 10978134) and Bekheirnia MR et al. JASN 2010. ACE inhibitor evidence per Gross O et al. Kidney Int 2012 EARLY PRO-TECT-Alport (PMID 22622453) and the German Alport Registry. Anti-GBM disease post-transplant per Kashtan CE. Transplantation 2006 and Pedchenko V et al. JASN 2010 (PMID 20705709). Newer therapies (SGLT2 inhibitors) per Boeckhaus J et al. Kidney Int 2023 / EMPA-KIDNEY in CKD broadly. Ocular findings per Savige J & Colville D. Pediatr Nephrol 2009 (PMID 19082630). Audiology timing per Alves FR & de Lima ALL. Braz J Otorhinolaryngol 2008 and Kashtan reviews.

h1Title: Alport Syndrome: Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: Alport syndrome is a hereditary disorder of type IV collagen that disrupts the structure of glomerular basement membranes, cochlear membranes, and ocular tissues, producing progressive kidney failure, bilateral sensorineural hearing loss, and characteristic eye findings. Estimated prevalence is roughly 1 in 5,000 to 1 in 50,000 depending on case definition, making Alport syndrome one of the most common hereditary kidney diseases after autosomal dominant polycystic kidney disease. Three inheritance patterns are recognized: X-linked (80-85% of cases, caused by COL4A5 mutations), autosomal recessive (10-15%, COL4A3 or COL4A4 homozygous or compound heterozygous mutations), and autosomal dominant (around 5%, COL4A3 or COL4A4 heterozygous mutations). The classic presentation in affected males with X-linked disease is persistent microscopic hematuria from infancy, episodic gross hematuria with intercurrent infections in childhood, progressive proteinuria from adolescence, and end-stage kidney disease by the third to fifth decade. Bilateral sensorineural hearing loss develops in adolescence and progresses through adulthood, and characteristic ocular signs (anterior lenticonus, dot-and-fleck retinopathy, posterior polymorphous corneal dystrophy) appear in advanced disease. Early diagnosis enables timely treatment with ACE inhibitors or ARBs, which delay end-stage kidney disease by approximately 10 years when started in childhood. Kidney transplantation is highly successful, with rare (around 5-10%) post-transplant anti-glomerular basement membrane disease in patients with absent COL4A5 protein.

keyStats.prevalence: Approximately 1 in 5,000 to 1 in 50,000 (depending on case definition); accounts for 1-2% of patients with end-stage kidney disease in registries
keyStats.demographics: X-linked Alport: males more severely affected; autosomal recessive and dominant: both sexes equally affected
keyStats.avgAge: Hematuria detectable from infancy; sensorineural hearing loss typically appears in adolescence; kidney failure age 20-40 in untreated males with X-linked disease
keyStats.globalCases: Estimated 600,000-1.5 million people affected globally; 1-2% of all dialysis patients have Alport syndrome

definition: Alport syndrome (ICD-10: Q87.81) is a heritable disease of the alpha-3/alpha-4/alpha-5 chains of type IV collagen, the structural network protein of basement membranes in the glomerulus, cochlea, ocular structures, and other tissues. Three inheritance patterns are now recognized as Alport syndrome under the 2019 international consensus classification: X-linked Alport syndrome (XLAS, around 80-85% of cases) caused by hemizygous mutations in COL4A5 in males or heterozygous mutations in females; autosomal recessive Alport syndrome (ARAS, around 10-15%) caused by homozygous or compound heterozygous mutations in COL4A3 or COL4A4; and autosomal dominant Alport syndrome (ADAS, around 5%) caused by heterozygous mutations in COL4A3 or COL4A4. What was previously termed 'thin basement membrane nephropathy' or 'benign familial hematuria' is now reclassified within the ADAS spectrum since these patients harbor heterozygous COL4A3 or COL4A4 mutations and a substantial subset develop late kidney failure. Disrupted assembly of the alpha-3/4/5 type IV collagen network destabilizes the glomerular basement membrane, leading to progressive splitting, lamellation, and thinning visible on electron microscopy. Loss of cochlear basement membrane integrity produces bilateral progressive sensorineural hearing loss, and disordered lens-capsule collagen causes anterior lenticonus. The disease is managed primarily by nephrology, with genetics, audiology, ophthalmology, and pediatric subspecialties involved depending on age and presentation.

typesClassification.description: 80-85% of cases. Males are severely affected with progressive kidney failure, hearing loss, and ocular signs; the disease progresses to end-stage kidney disease by age 20-40 in untreated patients. Heterozygous females have variable phenotype — most have isolated microscopic hematuria, but roughly 30% develop hearing loss by age 50 and 12-25% progress to ESKD.

typesClassification.type: Autosomal recessive Alport syndrome (ARAS, COL4A3 or COL4A4 biallelic)
typesClassification.description: 10-15% of cases. Both sexes equally severely affected, with progression to ESKD typically by adolescence or early adulthood. Often arises in consanguineous families. Hearing loss and ocular signs develop in childhood.

typesClassification.type: Autosomal dominant Alport syndrome (ADAS, COL4A3 or COL4A4 monoallelic)
typesClassification.description: Around 5% of clinically diagnosed cases but probably much more common given inclusion of historically labelled 'thin basement membrane disease'. Variable severity; many patients develop CKD over decades but progression to ESKD is slower than in XLAS or ARAS. Hearing loss and ocular signs less common.

Most have persistent microscopic hematuria from childhood; many remain otherwise well. Approximately 12-25% develop progressive CKD or ESKD by mid-life, and 30% develop hearing loss by age 50. All carriers should be monitored and offered ACE inhibitor/ARB therapy if proteinuria develops.

Rare. Large COL4A5/COL4A6 contiguous deletions cause Alport syndrome plus leiomyomatosis of the esophagus, tracheobronchial tree, and female genital tract. Presents with dysphagia, respiratory symptoms, and vulvar masses in addition to nephropathy.

primarySymptoms: Persistent microscopic hematuria detectable from infancy on urine dipstick or microscopy — typically the first laboratory abnormality and present in 100% of affected males and most females with X-linked disease.
Episodes of gross (visible) hematuria during or shortly after upper respiratory infections in children, lasting hours to days, often the trigger for diagnosis.

Progressive proteinuria starting in late childhood or adolescence in males with X-linked disease and earlier in autosomal recessive forms; quantitatively from low-grade albuminuria to nephrotic-range proteinuria.
Hypertension developing during childhood or adolescence, particularly with rising proteinuria.
Bilateral progressive high-frequency sensorineural hearing loss starting in adolescence in males with X-linked disease and in childhood in autosomal recessive disease.
Subjective hearing difficulty noticed first in noisy environments and on the telephone; eventually requires hearing aids.

Anterior lenticonus — protrusion of the central anterior lens surface into the anterior chamber — visible on slit-lamp examination in 60-70% of males with X-linked disease and pathognomonic for Alport syndrome.
Dot-and-fleck retinopathy: whitish or yellowish flecks in the macula, with sparing of the central fovea — present in 70-85% of XLAS males.
Posterior polymorphous corneal dystrophy with vesicle-like changes in the corneal endothelium.
Recurrent corneal erosions causing painful red eye.
Symptoms of advanced CKD (fatigue, edema, anemia, anorexia, pruritus) developing during adolescence or early adulthood in untreated patients.
Dysphagia, respiratory wheeze, or vulvar mass in rare Alport syndrome with diffuse leiomyomatosis.
earlyWarningSigns: Persistent microscopic hematuria in a child with a family history of kidney disease, hearing loss, or unexplained adult-onset kidney failure
Mild proteinuria appearing on routine urine testing in an asymptomatic child or adolescent
Bilateral high-frequency sensorineural hearing loss in a child or adolescent with no obvious cause
Recurrent gross hematuria with upper respiratory infections in childhood
First-degree relative with Alport syndrome — all children should be offered urinalysis, blood pressure, and genetic counseling
emergencySigns: Rapidly rising creatinine in a young patient with known or suspected Alport — exclude superimposed acute kidney injury (NSAIDs, dehydration, contrast nephropathy)
Severe hypertension or hypertensive emergency with target organ damage
Acute oliguria, fluid overload, or hyperkalemia — requires urgent renal evaluation
Sudden vision loss — exclude retinal hemorrhage or detachment, particularly in advanced disease
Anti-glomerular basement membrane disease in a kidney allograft post-transplant — rare but can be rapidly progressive

causes.cause: Mutations in COL4A5 on the X chromosome (X-linked Alport, ~80-85%)
causes.description: Hemizygous COL4A5 mutations cause severe disease in males. Heterozygous COL4A5 mutations cause variable disease in females depending on X-inactivation pattern. Mutation types include missense (often associated with later progression), splice-site, nonsense, frameshift, and large deletions (the latter associated with worse phenotype and rare diffuse leiomyomatosis).

causes.cause: Mutations in COL4A3 or COL4A4 on chromosome 2 (autosomal recessive ~10-15%, autosomal dominant ~5%)
causes.description: Homozygous or compound heterozygous mutations in COL4A3 or COL4A4 cause autosomal recessive Alport syndrome with severe disease in both sexes. Heterozygous mutations cause autosomal dominant Alport (formerly thin basement membrane nephropathy) with milder course but lifetime risk of CKD.

causes.cause: Disrupted assembly of the alpha-3-alpha-4-alpha-5 type IV collagen network
causes.description: Normal glomerular basement membrane uses an alpha-3-alpha-4-alpha-5 collagen IV trimer (rather than the alpha-1-alpha-1-alpha-2 trimer found in other tissues). When any of these three chains is defective, the network cannot assemble correctly, leaving the GBM mechanically vulnerable and prone to progressive splitting, lamellation, and breakdown.
Approximately 10-15% of Alport cases arise from de novo mutations without prior family history. Mosaic mutations (only in some cells) can produce milder phenotypes.

Within families carrying the same primary mutation, the rate of CKD progression varies, suggesting modifier genes and environmental influences (hypertension, obesity, nephrotoxic drug exposure, smoking) that accelerate progression.

riskFactors.factor: Family history of Alport syndrome or unexplained early kidney failure
riskFactors.description: All first-degree relatives of an affected individual should be screened with urinalysis, blood pressure, audiology, and genetic counseling. Half of relatives may be affected in autosomal dominant patterns; X-linked has its own inheritance pattern.
Males with X-linked COL4A5 mutations have severe disease; heterozygous females have variable disease.
Autosomal recessive Alport is more common in consanguineous populations and in genetic isolates.
Any child with persistent microscopic hematuria (especially with a family history of kidney disease or hearing loss) should be evaluated for Alport syndrome.
Hypertension and obesity accelerate CKD progression in Alport. Aggressive blood pressure control and weight management improve outcomes.

riskFactors.factor: NSAID use, smoking, and other nephrotoxins
riskFactors.description: Avoidance of nephrotoxic drugs and smoking cessation reduce additional kidney damage.

affectedDemographics: Geographic distribution: Alport syndrome occurs worldwide in all ethnic groups; autosomal recessive forms are more common in regions with consanguineous marriages (Middle East, North Africa, parts of South Asia).
Sex distribution: X-linked Alport affects males much more severely; females are typically less affected but still carry lifetime risk of CKD and hearing loss.

Age at end-stage kidney disease: untreated males with X-linked Alport typically reach ESKD between age 20 and 40; autosomal recessive disease tends to progress faster, often in adolescence; autosomal dominant disease typically progresses slower (sometimes into older age).
Among patients on dialysis or transplant waiting lists, Alport syndrome accounts for roughly 1-2% of cases.
Familial clustering is the norm — most cases have a known family history of hereditary nephritis, hearing loss, or unexplained early kidney failure.
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis combines clinical, laboratory, and genetic data. The classical workup begins with urinalysis (persistent microscopic hematuria, with or without proteinuria) and family history. Audiometry detects high-frequency bilateral sensorineural hearing loss; slit-lamp and retinal examination identify lenticonus, dot-and-fleck retinopathy, and corneal dystrophy. Renal function (creatinine, eGFR), urine albumin-to-creatinine ratio, and blood pressure document disease activity and progression. Genetic testing — typically a panel of COL4A3, COL4A4, and COL4A5 by next-generation sequencing — is now the diagnostic gold standard and has replaced biopsy in many centres for initial diagnosis. Kidney biopsy remains useful when genetic testing is inconclusive or when other diagnoses must be excluded; electron microscopy shows the characteristic lamellation, splitting, and thickness variation of the glomerular basement membrane (often with thin segments alternating with thickened lamellated segments). Immunostaining for collagen IV alpha-3, alpha-4, and alpha-5 chains shows characteristic patterns: total absence of alpha-3, alpha-4, and alpha-5 in males with X-linked Alport; absence of alpha-3, alpha-4, and alpha-5 in autosomal recessive Alport; segmental loss in carrier females; and normal staining in autosomal dominant Alport. Skin biopsy with COL4A5 immunostaining is a non-invasive alternative for X-linked Alport. Genetic counseling is offered to all patients and at-risk relatives. Diagnostic criteria (revised in 2019 by Savige et al.) include any one of: pathogenic COL4A3/4/5 variant, characteristic glomerular basement membrane changes on EM, or characteristic clinical features (hematuria with family history, hearing loss, lenticonus, or retinopathy).

diagnosticTests.test: Urinalysis with microscopy (red blood cells, casts) and urine protein:creatinine ratio
diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects persistent microscopic hematuria and quantifies proteinuria — the earliest manifestations of Alport
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard urine sample; result within hours. Microscopic hematuria persists indefinitely; proteinuria progressively rises with disease activity.

diagnosticTests.test: Genetic testing — next-generation sequencing of COL4A3, COL4A4, COL4A5 panel
diagnosticTests.purpose: Confirms diagnosis, identifies inheritance pattern, informs family screening and reproductive counseling
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Blood sample sent to a clinical genetics laboratory; result within 4-12 weeks. Now considered the diagnostic gold standard for most patients.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects characteristic bilateral high-frequency sensorineural hearing loss
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Painless test in a sound-treated booth; results immediate. Repeated periodically through childhood and adulthood.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies anterior lenticonus, dot-and-fleck retinopathy, posterior polymorphous corneal dystrophy
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard ophthalmology consultation with pupil dilation. Performed in childhood and repeated at intervals.

diagnosticTests.test: Kidney biopsy with light, immunofluorescence, and electron microscopy
diagnosticTests.purpose: Confirms diagnosis when genetic testing is unavailable or inconclusive; visualizes characteristic GBM lamellation and splitting; excludes other glomerular diseases
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Outpatient or day-case procedure under local anesthesia and ultrasound guidance. 4-6 hour observation afterwards. Result within 1-2 weeks.

diagnosticTests.test: Skin biopsy with collagen IV alpha-5 immunostaining
diagnosticTests.purpose: Non-invasive alternative to kidney biopsy for X-linked Alport in males (absent staining) and carrier females (segmental staining)
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Outpatient 4 mm punch biopsy under local anesthesia; result within 2-3 weeks.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects hypertension, which accelerates Alport progression
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Routine clinic measurement plus 24-hour ambulatory monitoring in selected patients.

treatmentOverview: Treatment of Alport syndrome aims to slow progression of chronic kidney disease, manage hearing loss, identify and treat ocular complications, and provide reproductive counseling. The cornerstone of medical management is renin-angiotensin system blockade with ACE inhibitors (ramipril, enalapril, lisinopril) or angiotensin receptor blockers (losartan, irbesartan), titrated to maximally tolerated doses based on blood pressure, eGFR, and serum potassium. Long-term observational studies and the EARLY PRO-TECT-Alport trial demonstrate that ACE inhibitor therapy started in childhood delays the onset of end-stage kidney disease by approximately 10 years compared with untreated historical controls. SGLT2 inhibitors (dapagliflozin, empagliflozin) reduce proteinuria and slow CKD progression across causes of chronic kidney disease and are increasingly used in adult Alport patients with eGFR above 25-30 mL/min/1.73m² based on the EMPA-KIDNEY and DAPA-CKD trials. Aldosterone antagonists (spironolactone, eplerenone) can be added in patients with persistent proteinuria despite ACE inhibitor or ARB therapy if potassium and eGFR allow. Blood pressure should be controlled to target under 130/80 mmHg (under 120/80 mmHg in younger patients tolerating it). Nephrotoxic drugs (NSAIDs, aminoglycosides) should be avoided. Hearing loss is managed with audiology follow-up, hearing aids, and (in advanced cases) cochlear implants. Lenticonus is corrected by lens replacement when visually significant. Patients should receive genetic counseling and family screening; prenatal and preimplantation genetic testing are options for affected families. Once ESKD develops, kidney transplantation offers excellent outcomes, with 5-year graft survival of 85-90% — equivalent to or better than transplant outcomes in patients with other diseases. A small minority (around 5-10%) of male X-linked Alport patients who lack the COL4A5 protein develop anti-glomerular basement membrane disease in the kidney allograft, presenting weeks to years after transplant and requiring plasmapheresis and immunosuppression. This complication is rare in autosomal recessive and dominant forms. Investigational therapies in late-phase clinical trials include atrasentan (endothelin receptor antagonist), bardoxolone methyl (Nrf2 activator — earlier trial failed but is being re-evaluated in selected populations), exon-skipping antisense oligonucleotides, and gene therapy. Cardiovascular risk reduction (statin, smoking cessation, weight management) is essential because Alport patients have elevated cardiovascular mortality similar to other CKD populations.

medicalTreatments.name: ACE inhibitor (ramipril, enalapril, lisinopril) — start at low dose in childhood, titrate to maximum tolerated
medicalTreatments.description: First-line therapy for all patients with detectable proteinuria or albuminuria. Slows progression by reducing intraglomerular pressure and direct anti-fibrotic effects. Monitor serum potassium and creatinine.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Delays ESKD by approximately 10 years when started in childhood (German Alport Registry; EARLY PRO-TECT-Alport)

medicalTreatments.name: Angiotensin receptor blocker (losartan, irbesartan, candesartan)
medicalTreatments.description: Alternative or substitute for ACE inhibitor in case of cough or angioedema. Equivalent effectiveness. Combined ACE inhibitor and ARB therapy is generally avoided because of hyperkalemia and AKI risk.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Similar to ACE inhibitor; the choice is individualized by tolerability

medicalTreatments.name: SGLT2 inhibitor (dapagliflozin 10 mg or empagliflozin 10 mg orally daily)
medicalTreatments.description: Reduces proteinuria and slows CKD progression in adults with Alport-related kidney disease and eGFR above 25-30 mL/min/1.73m². Generally well-tolerated; monitor for genital mycotic infections and rare diabetic ketoacidosis.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: 30-40% reduction in CKD progression endpoints in CKD populations including Alport (extrapolated from DAPA-CKD and EMPA-KIDNEY)

medicalTreatments.name: Aldosterone antagonist (spironolactone 12.5-50 mg daily or eplerenone 25-50 mg daily)
medicalTreatments.description: Adjunct in patients with persistent proteinuria despite maximal ACE inhibitor or ARB. Monitor potassium carefully — adds hyperkalemia risk on top of RAAS blockade.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Additional 25-40% reduction in proteinuria in selected patients

medicalTreatments.name: Hearing aids and cochlear implants
medicalTreatments.description: Hearing aids for moderate sensorineural hearing loss; cochlear implants for severe-to-profound loss not improved with aids. Audiology follow-up every 12 months from childhood.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Substantial functional benefit in over 90% of patients with properly fitted hearing aids; cochlear implants restore measurable speech recognition

medicalTreatments.name: Antihypertensive combination therapy
medicalTreatments.description: Target blood pressure under 130/80 mmHg using ACE inhibitor/ARB as foundation, with calcium channel blockers, beta-blockers, or thiazide diuretics added as needed.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Slows CKD progression independent of proteinuria reduction

medicalTreatments.name: Erythropoiesis-stimulating agents and iron replacement
medicalTreatments.description: Treat anemia of CKD with iron repletion (oral or IV) and erythropoiesis-stimulating agents (epoetin, darbepoetin) at target hemoglobin 100-115 g/L. Particularly important in adolescents and adults approaching ESKD.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Improvement in symptoms (fatigue, exercise tolerance) and quality of life

medicalTreatments.name: Vitamin D, phosphate binders, and bicarbonate replacement
medicalTreatments.description: Management of secondary hyperparathyroidism, hyperphosphatemia, and metabolic acidosis as CKD progresses to stages 3b-5.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces bone disease, vascular calcification, and progression of CKD

surgicalOptions.name: Kidney transplantation (living or deceased donor)
surgicalOptions.description: Definitive treatment for ESKD. Preemptive transplantation (before dialysis) is preferred where feasible. Outcomes equal to or better than other indications. Care needed in selecting affected family members as donors — heterozygous female XLAS carriers may safely donate, but assessment of their own kidney function and risk of late progression is essential.
surgicalOptions.successRate: 5-year graft survival 85-90%; patient survival over 95% at 5 years

surgicalOptions.name: Lens replacement (clear lens extraction with intraocular lens implantation)
surgicalOptions.description: For visually significant anterior lenticonus. Standard cataract-style surgery with intraocular lens implantation.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Visual improvement in over 95% of operated patients

surgicalOptions.name: Cochlear implantation
surgicalOptions.description: For severe-to-profound sensorineural hearing loss unresponsive to hearing aids. Outcomes generally good, comparable to other adult cochlear implant indications.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Speech recognition restoration in over 80% of recipients at 12 months

alternativeTreatments.name: Investigational therapies (atrasentan, bardoxolone, exon-skipping oligonucleotides, gene therapy)
alternativeTreatments.description: Several phase 2/3 trials are evaluating endothelin receptor antagonists, Nrf2 activators, exon-skipping antisense oligonucleotides targeting specific COL4A5 mutations, and AAV-delivered gene therapy. None is approved as of 2026 but trial enrolment is encouraged at specialist centres.

alternativeTreatments.name: Mesenchymal stromal cell therapy
alternativeTreatments.description: Preclinical and early-phase clinical investigation of MSC therapy in Alport; not yet proven effective and not standard of care.

whySeeSpecialist: Diagnosis and management of Alport syndrome requires coordinated care from nephrology, clinical genetics, audiology, ophthalmology, and pediatrics. Specialist nephrology services with genetic kidney disease expertise should coordinate care, particularly at children's hospitals with specialty pediatric nephrology and at adult centres with hereditary kidney disease programmes.
doctorSelectionGuide: Choose a nephrologist with expertise in hereditary kidney disease, ideally affiliated with an Alport syndrome research network (e.g., the European Reference Network on Rare Kidney Diseases, US Alport Syndrome Treatments and Outcomes Registry, or national reference centres). Genetic counseling should be available within or alongside the nephrology service. For transplant care, choose a centre with experience in hereditary kidney disease and post-transplant anti-GBM disease monitoring.
hospitalCriteria: Nephrology service with hereditary kidney disease clinic
Clinical genetics service with COL4A3/4/5 testing access
Audiology and ophthalmology services for routine Alport screening
Renal transplant programme experienced in hereditary kidney disease
Multi-disciplinary team meetings for complex pediatric and adult cases
keyFacilities: Audiology and ophthalmology clinics integrated with nephrology
preventionStrategies: Family screening: all first-degree relatives of an affected individual should have urinalysis, blood pressure, and genetic counseling.
Early diagnosis and treatment: start ACE inhibitor or ARB therapy in childhood once persistent proteinuria or albuminuria is detected (or earlier in some protocols for boys with XLAS).
Tight blood pressure control to under 130/80 mmHg.
Avoid nephrotoxic drugs (NSAIDs, aminoglycosides, gadolinium-based contrast in advanced CKD).
Reproductive counseling: discuss prenatal testing, preimplantation genetic diagnosis, and donor-egg/donor-sperm options for affected families.
Stay up to date with audiology and ophthalmology surveillance every 12 months from childhood.
Maintain healthy weight, no smoking, and regular physical activity to reduce additional CKD risk factors.
lifestyleModifications: Adhere strictly to prescribed ACE inhibitor, ARB, or SGLT2 inhibitor therapy at the same time daily.
Avoid NSAIDs (ibuprofen, diclofenac, naproxen) and other nephrotoxic drugs — discuss any new medications with your nephrologist.
Limit dietary sodium to under 2 g/day to support blood pressure control.
Limit alcohol consumption; do not smoke.
Use compression-style hearing aids when prescribed and replace as recommended.
Attend regular nephrology, audiology, and ophthalmology follow-up.
Maintain physical activity 150 minutes per week unless contraindicated by advanced CKD.

recommended: Mediterranean-style diet rich in vegetables, fruit, whole grains, and lean protein
Adequate protein intake (1.0-1.2 g/kg/day) unless approaching ESKD
Plant-based protein sources (legumes, lentils) may slow CKD progression
Calcium-rich foods and vitamin D supplementation to maintain bone health
avoid: Excess phosphate (processed foods, cola, dairy) in advanced CKD
Excess potassium (bananas, oranges, tomatoes) in advanced CKD with hyperkalemia

exerciseGuidelines: Regular moderate aerobic exercise (150 minutes per week) and resistance training are beneficial. Avoid contact sports with severe thrombocytopenia. Once on dialysis, structured exercise programmes during sessions can improve cardiovascular outcomes and quality of life.
dailyManagement: Take ACE inhibitor or ARB at the same time daily.
Monitor blood pressure at home and report sustained elevation.
Use prescribed hearing aids during waking hours.
Apply sunglasses outdoors to reduce photophobia and protect lenses.
Attend lab monitoring (creatinine, eGFR, urine ACR, potassium) every 3-6 months.
Track family birthdays for cascade screening reminders.
Carry a medical alert ID stating Alport syndrome and current medications.
prognosis: Outcome in Alport syndrome depends primarily on inheritance pattern, genotype, sex, age at diagnosis, and access to effective treatment. Males with X-linked Alport historically progressed to end-stage kidney disease between ages 20 and 40; modern early treatment with ACE inhibitors or ARBs delays ESKD by approximately 10 years. Autosomal recessive disease progresses faster than X-linked. Autosomal dominant disease (formerly thin basement membrane disease) progresses more slowly, with many patients reaching ESKD only after age 60 if at all. Heterozygous female X-linked carriers have variable course: most maintain normal kidney function for life, but roughly 12-25% develop ESKD and 30% develop hearing loss by age 50. Bilateral progressive sensorineural hearing loss is managed effectively with modern hearing aids and cochlear implants. Ocular signs rarely cause major visual disability; lenticonus is correctable with lens replacement. Kidney transplant outcomes are excellent: 5-year graft survival 85-90% and patient survival over 95%. Approximately 5-10% of male X-linked patients lacking COL4A5 protein develop post-transplant anti-glomerular basement membrane disease, which requires plasmapheresis, immunosuppression, and sometimes retransplantation. Cardiovascular morbidity is the dominant non-renal cause of death and requires aggressive risk-factor management. Investigational therapies (SGLT2 inhibitors now standard adjuncts, atrasentan, exon-skipping therapy, gene therapy) hold promise for further delaying or potentially halting kidney disease in coming years.
recoveryTimeline: Alport is a lifelong condition rather than an acute illness. Hematuria persists indefinitely. ACE inhibitor or ARB therapy is lifelong. End-stage kidney disease typically occurs in adulthood; once on dialysis, transplant evaluation is started. Post-transplant: graft function recovers over days; immunosuppression is lifelong; routine surveillance every 3-12 months. Hearing loss requires audiology follow-up annually with hearing aid adjustments as needed.
complications: Progressive chronic kidney disease leading to end-stage kidney disease requiring dialysis or transplantation.
Anterior lenticonus, dot-and-fleck retinopathy, and corneal dystrophy.
Anemia of CKD with fatigue and reduced exercise tolerance.
Secondary hyperparathyroidism and CKD-related bone disease.
Post-transplant anti-glomerular basement membrane disease (5-10% of male X-linked Alport with absent COL4A5).
Reproductive concerns including inheritance to offspring and pregnancy-related kidney function decline in affected women.

supportResources.name: Alport Syndrome Foundation (US)
supportResources.description: US-based patient organization providing education, research support, and peer connections for families with Alport syndrome.

supportResources.name: Alport UK
supportResources.description: UK patient charity offering information, support groups, and advocacy for Alport syndrome.

supportResources.name: Alport Network Australasia
supportResources.description: Australian and New Zealand patient and research network for Alport syndrome.

supportResources.name: European Reference Network on Rare Kidney Diseases (ERKNet)
supportResources.description: EU network coordinating expert care for rare kidney diseases including Alport syndrome.

supportResources.name: US Alport Syndrome Treatments and Outcomes Registry (ASTOR)
supportResources.description: International registry tracking Alport syndrome patients to advance research and treatment.

confusedWithConditions.name: IgA Nephropathy
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Both can cause hematuria and proteinuria in young adults. IgA nephropathy is diagnosed by kidney biopsy with mesangial IgA deposition and is typically not familial in the same Mendelian pattern. Alport features family history, hearing loss, ocular signs, and COL4A3/4/5 mutations on genetic testing.

confusedWithConditions.name: Polycystic Kidney Disease
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: ADPKD presents with multiple bilateral renal cysts on imaging and produces hypertension, hematuria, and eventual ESKD over decades. Imaging is diagnostic. Alport syndrome lacks cysts and shows characteristic GBM changes on biopsy or COL4A3/4/5 mutations.

confusedWithConditions.name: Sensorineural Hearing Loss
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Isolated sensorineural hearing loss has many causes (noise, genetic, age-related). Alport-related hearing loss is bilateral, high-frequency, progressive, develops in adolescence, and is accompanied by kidney disease. Genetic testing identifies COL4A3/4/5 mutations.

confusedWithConditions.name: Chronic kidney disease
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Generic CKD has many causes; Alport syndrome is one specific cause. Family history, hearing loss, lenticonus, and COL4A3/4/5 mutations on genetic testing distinguish Alport from other CKD etiologies. Diagnosis is important because management and family counseling differ.

confusedWithConditions.name: End stage renal disease
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: ESKD is the structural endpoint of many kidney diseases. Alport syndrome leading to ESKD requires identification of the underlying genetic cause to enable family screening and appropriate transplant planning, including assessment of family donors and anti-GBM disease risk.

relatedConditions.name: Sensorineural Hearing Loss
relatedConditions.relevance: Cardinal extra-renal feature of Alport syndrome; routine audiology surveillance and hearing aid provision are integral to care.

relatedConditions.name: Chronic kidney disease
relatedConditions.relevance: Alport syndrome is a major hereditary cause of CKD progression in young adults.

relatedConditions.name: End stage renal disease
relatedConditions.relevance: Eventual outcome in untreated or severe Alport syndrome; transplant evaluation is part of standard care.

relatedConditions.name: IgA Nephropathy
relatedConditions.relevance: Important glomerular differential for persistent hematuria in young adults; distinguished by biopsy and genetic testing.

relatedConditions.name: Polycystic Kidney Disease
relatedConditions.relevance: Another hereditary kidney disease requiring family screening and counseling; distinguished by imaging.

faqs.question: What is Alport syndrome?
faqs.answer: Alport syndrome is a hereditary disease of type IV collagen that affects glomerular basement membranes, the inner ear, and the eye. It causes persistent microscopic hematuria from infancy, progressive kidney failure typically in young adulthood, bilateral sensorineural hearing loss, and characteristic ocular signs. Three inheritance patterns are recognized (X-linked, autosomal recessive, autosomal dominant).

faqs.question: How is Alport syndrome inherited?
faqs.answer: Alport has three inheritance patterns: X-linked (around 80-85%, COL4A5 on the X chromosome), autosomal recessive (around 10-15%, COL4A3 or COL4A4 biallelic mutations), and autosomal dominant (around 5%, COL4A3 or COL4A4 heterozygous mutations). Family history and genetic testing guide inheritance counseling.

faqs.question: What are the symptoms of Alport syndrome?
faqs.answer: Persistent microscopic hematuria from infancy, episodes of visible hematuria with infections in childhood, progressive proteinuria, hypertension, and chronic kidney disease progressing to end-stage kidney disease in young adulthood. Bilateral sensorineural hearing loss develops in adolescence, and ocular signs (lenticonus, dot-and-fleck retinopathy) appear in advanced disease.

faqs.question: Is Alport syndrome treatable?
faqs.answer: Yes. ACE inhibitors or angiotensin receptor blockers started early can delay end-stage kidney disease by approximately 10 years. SGLT2 inhibitors provide additional renal protection. Hearing aids and cochlear implants manage hearing loss. Kidney transplantation is highly successful when ESKD develops. Several investigational therapies are in late-phase trials.

faqs.question: How is Alport syndrome diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis combines clinical features (hematuria, family history, hearing loss, eye findings), genetic testing of COL4A3/COL4A4/COL4A5 (now first-line), and where needed, kidney biopsy with electron microscopy showing characteristic glomerular basement membrane changes. Audiology and ophthalmology evaluations complete the workup.

faqs.question: Is genetic testing necessary for Alport syndrome?
faqs.answer: Genetic testing is now the diagnostic gold standard for most patients with suspected Alport syndrome. Next-generation sequencing of COL4A3, COL4A4, and COL4A5 confirms diagnosis, identifies the inheritance pattern, informs family screening, and supports reproductive counseling. It often replaces kidney biopsy as the initial test.

faqs.question: Can women have Alport syndrome?
faqs.answer: Yes. Women with X-linked Alport are heterozygous carriers with variable disease — most have persistent hematuria; 12-25% develop progressive kidney disease and 30% develop hearing loss by age 50. Women with autosomal recessive or autosomal dominant Alport are affected as severely as men. All female relatives of affected individuals should be screened.

faqs.question: Can children with Alport syndrome lead normal lives?
faqs.answer: Yes, with early diagnosis and treatment. Children with Alport syndrome generally have normal growth, development, and school performance through childhood. Hearing aids may be needed in adolescence. Sport and physical activity are encouraged. Treatment with ACE inhibitors or ARBs from childhood delays kidney failure substantially.

faqs.question: When does kidney failure develop in Alport syndrome?
faqs.answer: Untreated males with X-linked Alport typically reach end-stage kidney disease between ages 20 and 40. Autosomal recessive disease progresses faster, often in adolescence. Autosomal dominant disease progresses more slowly, sometimes only in older age. Early ACE inhibitor or ARB therapy delays ESKD by roughly 10 years.

faqs.question: Is kidney transplant safe in Alport syndrome?
faqs.answer: Yes. Kidney transplantation has excellent outcomes in Alport syndrome — 5-year graft survival 85-90%. Living related donors can be used after careful assessment to ensure the donor does not have progressive Alport disease themselves. About 5-10% of male X-linked patients develop post-transplant anti-glomerular basement membrane disease.

faqs.question: Does Alport syndrome always cause deafness?
faqs.answer: Most males with X-linked or autosomal recessive Alport develop bilateral sensorineural hearing loss in adolescence or early adulthood. Females with X-linked carrier status develop hearing loss in about 30% by age 50. Hearing aids are effective; cochlear implants are used for profound loss.

faqs.question: What are the eye signs of Alport syndrome?
faqs.answer: Anterior lenticonus (protrusion of the central anterior lens surface) is virtually pathognomonic for Alport. Dot-and-fleck retinopathy (whitish or yellowish flecks around the macula) is also common. Posterior polymorphous corneal dystrophy and recurrent corneal erosions can cause painful red eye. Routine ophthalmology surveillance is standard.

faqs.question: Can Alport syndrome be cured?
faqs.answer: There is currently no cure for Alport syndrome, but progression can be slowed substantially with early ACE inhibitor or ARB therapy, SGLT2 inhibitors, and tight blood pressure control. Investigational therapies including endothelin antagonists, Nrf2 activators, exon-skipping antisense oligonucleotides, and gene therapy are in clinical trials.

faqs.question: Should family members be tested for Alport syndrome?
faqs.answer: Yes. All first-degree relatives of an affected individual should have urinalysis (for microscopic hematuria), blood pressure measurement, and ideally genetic counseling and testing. Audiology and ophthalmology screening are recommended for those with confirmed or suspected disease. Early diagnosis enables earlier treatment.

faqs.question: What is the difference between Alport syndrome and thin basement membrane nephropathy?
faqs.answer: Historical 'thin basement membrane nephropathy' is now recognized as a milder form of Alport syndrome (autosomal dominant Alport syndrome, ADAS) caused by heterozygous COL4A3 or COL4A4 mutations. Many of these patients develop CKD over decades and warrant similar monitoring and management to other Alport variants.

faqs.question: Is pregnancy safe with Alport syndrome?
faqs.answer: Pregnancy is generally safe in women with mild Alport syndrome and normal kidney function, although it may transiently accelerate kidney function decline in some. Preconception counseling with nephrology and obstetrics is essential. Preimplantation genetic diagnosis is available for families wishing to avoid transmission.

faqs.question: What is anti-GBM disease after Alport transplant?
faqs.answer: About 5-10% of male X-linked Alport patients who lack the COL4A5 protein develop anti-glomerular basement membrane disease in their kidney allograft, weeks to years after transplantation. The recipient's immune system recognizes the donor's normal COL4A5 as foreign. Treatment is plasmapheresis and immunosuppression; outcomes vary.

faqs.question: Should Alport patients avoid NSAIDs?
faqs.answer: Yes. Non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (ibuprofen, diclofenac, naproxen) reduce renal blood flow and accelerate kidney function decline. Patients with Alport syndrome should avoid them and discuss alternatives (paracetamol, topical agents) with their nephrologist.

faqs.question: Can Alport syndrome be detected before birth?
faqs.answer: Yes. Prenatal genetic testing by chorionic villus sampling or amniocentesis is available for families with a known causative mutation. Preimplantation genetic diagnosis with in-vitro fertilization is also an option. Genetic counseling discusses these options for affected families.

faqs.question: Is Alport syndrome more common in certain populations?
faqs.answer: Alport syndrome occurs worldwide in all ethnic groups. Autosomal recessive forms are more common in regions with consanguineous marriages (Middle East, North Africa, parts of South Asia). Founder mutations have been identified in specific populations, sometimes leading to local clustering.

faqs.question: What new treatments are being developed for Alport syndrome?
faqs.answer: Several agents are in late-phase clinical trials: endothelin receptor antagonists (atrasentan), Nrf2 activators (bardoxolone), exon-skipping antisense oligonucleotides targeting specific COL4A5 mutations, and AAV-delivered gene therapy. SGLT2 inhibitors are increasingly used as adjunct therapy. Patients should consider clinical trial enrollment at specialist centres.
